segments.title: Conwy Castle controls port and river
segments.summary: The Conwy Castle lecture treats the fortress as a maritime strongpoint controlling a port, town, river crossing, and sea-supply route.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames Conwy as one of the clearest examples of the Ring of Iron's maritime logic. The castle has its own port, dominates the town's waterfront, and sits on a promontory that makes resupply hard to prevent without a navy. The later strategic recap connects that setting to control of the river crossing, local goods movement, and the problem of leaving a sea-defended fortress in an army's rear.

evidence.note: The opening uses the diorama to describe Conwy's maritime character, including the town port, the castle's separate port, protective fire over the town, and sea-backed resupply.
The later recap explains why Conwy matters: it controls a river crossing, remains easy to resupply by sea, affects local goods movement, and is difficult to leave untended.
